name: Canada Interim Housing Assistance Program (IHAP)
description: The Interim Housing Assistance Program (IHAP) provides federal funding to provinces, municipalities, and non-profit organizations to support housing solutions for asylum claimants in Canada. The program helps asylum claimants achieve housing independence through interim shelter, transitional housing, and related wraparound services. Budget 2024 allocated $1.1 billion over three years through 2026-2027, with the federal government covering up to 75% of costs for sustainable housing approaches. Eligible projects include emergency shelters, transitional housing, housing navigation services, and supports that help asylum claimants transition to permanent housing.
eligibility: Asylum claimants in Canada; funding flows through provincial/territorial/municipal governments and non-profit organizations serving asylum claimants
amount: $1.1 billion total (2024-2027); federal covers up to 75% of eligible project costs
applicationProcess: Provincial, territorial, or municipal governments and non-profit organizations apply through IRCC for grant or contribution funding agreements
documentsRequired: Project proposal, budget, demonstration of matching provincial/territorial/municipal funding (minimum 25-50%)

name: Immigration Equality — Free Legal Services for LGBTQ+ Asylum Seekers
description: Immigration Equality provides free direct legal services to LGBTQ+ and HIV-positive immigrants and asylum seekers in the United States. The organization connects over 750 individuals annually with pro bono attorneys through its national network of law firm partners. Services include asylum representation, deportation defense, family-based petitions, adjustment of status, and assistance with USCIS applications. Immigration Equality also operates a free legal helpline and provides know-your-rights resources in multiple languages for LGBTQ+ immigrants facing persecution based on sexual orientation, gender identity, or HIV status.
eligibility: LGBTQ+ and HIV-positive immigrants, asylum seekers, and refugees in the United States regardless of immigration status
applicationProcess: Contact Immigration Equality through their website intake form or legal helpline to request legal assistance; cases are matched with pro bono attorneys
geographicScope: National (headquartered in New York; serves clients nationwide through pro bono attorney network)
documentsRequired: Identification documents, evidence of persecution based on LGBTQ+ identity or HIV status, any existing immigration case documents

name: Global Refuge Immigration Legal Services
organization: Global Refuge (formerly Lutheran Immigration and Refugee Service)
description: Global Refuge, formerly known as Lutheran Immigration and Refugee Service (LIRS), provides comprehensive immigration legal services to refugees, asylum seekers, unaccompanied children, and other vulnerable immigrants across the United States. Founded in 1939, the organization offers direct legal representation, legal orientations for detained individuals, pro bono attorney coordination, and support for immigrant families navigating the U.S. immigration system. Global Refuge operates through a network of local affiliates and partners in communities across the country, helping immigrants with asylum applications, family reunification, Special Immigrant Visa cases, and humanitarian relief.
eligibility: Refugees, asylum seekers, unaccompanied children, immigrants and their families in the United States; priority given to vulnerable populations
applicationProcess: Contact Global Refuge or local affiliate organizations for intake; services provided through network of offices and pro bono attorneys nationwide
geographicScope: National (offices and affiliates across multiple U.S. states)
documentsRequired: Identification, immigration documents, evidence supporting immigration case (varies by case type)

name: FEMA Shelter and Services Program (SSP)
description: The FEMA Shelter and Services Program (SSP) provides federal funding to state, local, and tribal governments and non-profit organizations to support communities receiving non-citizen migrants. The program funds emergency shelter, food, transportation to final destinations within the U.S., acute medical care, crisis counseling, and other essential social services. SSP was established by Congress to help offset the costs that local communities incur when providing humanitarian assistance to newly arrived migrants. Funding is distributed through a competitive process to eligible jurisdictions and organizations experiencing significant migrant arrivals.
eligibility: Non-citizen migrants in the United States; funding distributed through state/local governments and non-profit organizations serving migrant populations
amount: Varies by jurisdiction; total program funding set by annual Congressional appropriation
applicationProcess: Eligible state, local, and tribal governments and non-profits apply through FEMA; individual migrants access services through local partner organizations
documentsRequired: None for individual migrants accessing services; organizations must submit grant applications with projected costs and migrant arrival data

name: EU Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund (AMIF 2021-2027)
organization: European Commission — DG Migration and Home Affairs
description: The Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund (AMIF) is the European Union's primary funding instrument for asylum, migration, and integration policies. With a total budget of EUR 10.94 billion for 2021-2027, AMIF supports EU member states in managing migration effectively through funding for reception conditions, asylum procedures, integration of third-country nationals, and return operations. The fund finances language training, employment support, housing assistance, legal counseling, and social orientation programs for refugees and migrants across all 27 EU member states. Organizations and government bodies can access AMIF funding through national programs or directly through EU-level calls for proposals.
eligibility: Third-country nationals, refugees, asylum seekers, and beneficiaries of international protection in EU member states; funding available to government bodies, NGOs, and international organizations
amount: EUR 10.94 billion total (2021-2027); individual project grants vary
applicationProcess: Apply through national AMIF programs managed by EU member state authorities, or respond to EU-level calls for proposals published on the European Commission funding portal
documentsRequired: Project proposal, budget plan, organizational capacity documentation; requirements vary by national program and EU-level calls

name: Asylum Seeker Advocacy Project (ASAP) — Free Legal Guidance
description: The Asylum Seeker Advocacy Project (ASAP) provides free legal guidance and support to asylum seekers and immigrants across the United States through its online platform and community-based services. ASAP offers free membership that includes access to expert immigration attorneys who answer legal questions, provide guidance on asylum applications, and help immigrants understand their rights. The organization also operates community navigators who provide in-person support and connects members with local legal service providers. ASAP's digital platform delivers multilingual know-your-rights information and court preparation resources accessible from anywhere in the country.
eligibility: Asylum seekers and immigrants in the United States; free membership open to all regardless of immigration status
amount: Free legal guidance and support services
applicationProcess: Sign up for free ASAP membership online at asaptogether.org; submit legal questions through the platform to receive guidance from immigration attorneys
geographicScope: National (online platform accessible nationwide; community navigators in select cities)
documentsRequired: None required for membership; relevant immigration documents helpful when asking legal questions

name: California Immigration Services Funding (ISF) Program
description: The California Immigration Services Funding (ISF) Program provides state funding to qualified nonprofit organizations to deliver immigration legal services, naturalization assistance, and education to immigrants in California. The program funds services including legal screenings, assistance with naturalization applications, DACA renewals, representation in immigration court, community education on immigrant rights, and anti-fraud consumer protection. ISF is one of the largest state-funded immigration legal services programs in the United States, supporting a network of community-based organizations across all regions of California to help immigrants obtain legal status and integrate into their communities.
eligibility: Immigrants in California seeking legal services, naturalization assistance, or immigration education; services delivered through qualified nonprofit organizations
amount: Varies; California allocates tens of millions annually to the ISF program for distribution to qualified organizations
applicationProcess: Immigrants access services by contacting ISF-funded nonprofit organizations in their area; organizations apply to CDSS through competitive grant process
documentsRequired: Identification, any existing immigration documents; specific requirements vary by service type and provider organization
